concepts: threads within a process share memory
threads have lower creation and context-switch overhead
process isolation provides stronger fault isolation
questionPatterns: What's the difference between a process and a thread?
Why are threads generally considered lighter than processes?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes separate versus shared memory
explains the relative overhead of processes and threads
understands the isolation versus communication tradeoff

concepts: CPU switches execution between processes or threads
execution state must be saved and restored
questionPatterns: What happens during a context switch?
Why can having too many threads hurt performance?
evaluationCriteria: explains saving and restoring execution state
recognizes context switching has computational overhead
connects excessive concurrency to possible performance degradation

questionPatterns: What's the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling?
Why is Round Robin commonly used in time-sharing systems?
evaluationCriteria: explains major scheduling approaches correctly
connects Round Robin to time slices and fairness
recognizes starvation as a possible scheduling problem

concepts: concurrency does not necessarily mean parallel execution
race conditions can occur without coordination
questionPatterns: What's the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
What problems can occur when multiple threads modify shared data?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes concurrency from parallelism
identifies race conditions as a shared-state problem
understands synchronization is required around critical shared resources

concepts: result depends on unpredictable execution ordering
critical section accesses shared mutable state
race conditions can produce intermittent bugs
questionPatterns: What is a race condition?
Two threads increment the same counter. Why might the final value be incorrect?
evaluationCriteria: explains that unsynchronized operations can interleave
identifies shared mutable state as the core issue
proposes appropriate synchronization

concepts: binary ownership model for locking critical sections
semaphores can limit access to a finite resource pool
questionPatterns: What's the difference between a mutex and a semaphore?
When would you use a counting semaphore instead of a mutex?
evaluationCriteria: correctly connects mutexes to exclusive access
understands semaphores maintain available permits
provides a reasonable resource-pool use case

questionPatterns: What conditions must exist for a deadlock to occur?
How could two threads holding different locks deadlock each other?
evaluationCriteria: identifies the four Coffman conditions
can describe a concrete circular-wait scenario
suggests breaking at least one required condition as prevention

concepts: deadlock means blocked participants cannot make progress
starvation means one participant repeatedly fails to obtain resources
questionPatterns: What's the difference between deadlock and starvation?
Can a system continue running while one process is starving?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes total circular blocking from indefinite resource denial
understands other processes may continue during starvation
connects aging to starvation prevention

concepts: provides processes with a logical address space
maps virtual addresses to physical memory
allows programs larger than available physical memory
questionPatterns: What is virtual memory and why do operating systems use it?
Can a program use more memory than the available RAM?
evaluationCriteria: explains logical versus physical memory
understands memory can be backed by secondary storage
mentions isolation as an important benefit

concepts: memory divided into fixed-size pages and frames
page tables map virtual pages to physical frames
page fault occurs when required page is not currently available in physical memory
operating system loads the required page before execution continues
questionPatterns: What happens when a page fault occurs?
What's the purpose of a page table?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains virtual-to-physical address mapping
understands a page fault may require loading data from secondary storage
recognizes page faults are significantly more expensive than normal memory access

concepts: FIFO replaces the oldest loaded page
LRU approximates replacing the least recently used page
optimal replacement removes the page needed furthest in the future
page replacement affects page fault frequency
questionPatterns: What's the difference between FIFO and LRU page replacement?
Why can't the optimal page replacement algorithm be implemented perfectly in practice?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains major replacement strategies
understands optimal replacement requires future knowledge
connects poor replacement decisions to increased page faults

concepts: system spends excessive time handling page faults
insufficient physical frames for active working sets
frequent swapping reduces useful CPU work
questionPatterns: What is thrashing in an operating system?
Why can adding more processes sometimes reduce overall system performance?
evaluationCriteria: connects thrashing to excessive paging
understands insufficient memory for active working sets as a cause
recognizes the system remains busy while doing little useful work

concepts: stack commonly stores function call frames and local variables
heap is used for dynamically allocated objects
stack allocation follows structured lifetime rules
heap allocation requires explicit or automatic memory management depending on language
questionPatterns: What's the difference between stack and heap memory?
Why is stack allocation generally faster than heap allocation?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes common stack and heap usage
understands stack lifetime follows function execution
recognizes dynamic allocation requires additional management

concepts: TCP provides reliable ordered byte delivery
UDP is connectionless and does not guarantee delivery or ordering
questionPatterns: What's the difference between TCP and UDP?
Would you choose TCP or UDP for a live video call, and why?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes reliability and ordering guarantees
understands UDP generally has lower protocol overhead
selects protocols based on application requirements rather than claiming one is always better

questionPatterns: Walk me through the TCP three-way handshake.
Why does TCP need a handshake before application data is exchanged?
evaluationCriteria: correctly states SYN, SYN-ACK and ACK sequence
understands both endpoints establish connection state
connects the handshake to reliable communication setup

concepts: application, transport, network and link responsibilities
TCP and UDP operate at transport layer
IP handles network addressing and routing
questionPatterns: At which layers do HTTP, TCP and IP operate?
Why are networking protocols organized into layers?
evaluationCriteria: places common protocols in the correct conceptual layers
understands separation of responsibilities
can trace a web request through major layers at a high level

concepts: domain names mapped to IP addresses
root, TLD and authoritative name servers
questionPatterns: What happens when you enter a domain name into your browser and the DNS result isn't cached?
Why does DNS use caching?
evaluationCriteria: explains DNS as name-to-address resolution
understands recursive resolution at a high level
connects caching to lower latency and reduced DNS traffic

questionPatterns: What happens after you type a URL into a browser and press Enter?
Walk me through how a request reaches a web server.
evaluationCriteria: provides a logically ordered high-level flow
includes DNS and network connection establishment
recognizes HTTPS introduces TLS before protected HTTP communication

concepts: HTTPS is HTTP protected using TLS
authentication certificates help verify server identity
questionPatterns: What's the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
What security guarantees does HTTPS provide?
evaluationCriteria: explains that HTTPS uses TLS rather than being an unrelated protocol
mentions encryption and server authentication
understands HTTPS protects data in transit

concepts: POST commonly creates or triggers non-idempotent operations
PUT commonly replaces a resource representation
idempotency means repeating an operation has the same intended effect as performing it once
questionPatterns: What's the difference between PUT and PATCH?
What does it mean for an HTTP method to be idempotent?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes common HTTP method semantics
explains idempotency based on repeated effect
recognizes GET, PUT and DELETE are defined as idempotent while POST generally is not

questionPatterns: What's the difference between 401 and 403?
What status code would you return after successfully creating a resource?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes authentication failure from insufficient authorization
uses 201 appropriately for resource creation
understands status-code categories rather than only memorizing numbers

concepts: cookies are browser-managed storage sent according to cookie rules
server-side sessions store authentication state on the server
session identifiers can be stored in cookies
tokens can carry claims and be validated without traditional server-side session storage
questionPatterns: What's the difference between cookie-based sessions and token-based authentication?
Are cookies and sessions the same thing?
evaluationCriteria: correctly separates the cookie storage/transport mechanism from server-side session state
understands session identifiers can be carried in cookies
avoids claiming token authentication is automatically more secure

concepts: role and permission checks are authorization concerns
questionPatterns: What's the difference between authentication and authorization?
A logged-in user tries to access an admin endpoint. Which security concern decides whether access is allowed?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes identity verification from permission checking
provides a valid example
understands being authenticated does not imply access to every resource

questionPatterns: What makes an API RESTful?
How would you design endpoints for managing users and their orders?
evaluationCriteria: uses resource-oriented endpoint naming
applies HTTP methods appropriately
understands statelessness
avoids putting every operation into verb-heavy URL paths

questionPatterns: What are the four pillars of object-oriented programming?
Give a practical example of each major OOP principle.
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains all four concepts
uses examples rather than only memorized definitions
distinguishes abstraction from encapsulation

concepts: abstraction hides unnecessary implementation complexity behind useful interfaces
encapsulation groups state and behavior while controlling access
both reduce complexity in different ways
questionPatterns: What's the difference between abstraction and encapsulation?
Give a practical example where both are used together.
evaluationCriteria: does not treat the two concepts as identical
explains abstraction in terms of exposing essential behavior
explains encapsulation in terms of controlling internal state and implementation

concepts: composition builds behavior from contained collaborators
deep inheritance hierarchies can become rigid
questionPatterns: When would you prefer composition over inheritance?
Why is 'favor composition over inheritance' common design advice?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes is-a from has-a relationships
recognizes composition's flexibility
does not claim inheritance is always bad

concepts: method overloading where supported as compile-time polymorphism
questionPatterns: What is polymorphism? Give a practical example.
What's the difference between method overloading and overriding?
evaluationCriteria: explains polymorphism through interchangeable behavior
correctly distinguishes overriding from overloading
provides a meaningful example

questionPatterns: What are the SOLID principles and why are they useful?
Give an example of violating the Single Responsibility Principle.
evaluationCriteria: explains the purpose rather than only expanding the acronym
can explain at least several principles with examples
connects SOLID to maintainability and change management

concepts: low coupling reduces dependencies between modules
high cohesion keeps related responsibilities together
loosely coupled components are easier to replace and test
questionPatterns: What do low coupling and high cohesion mean?
Why are they considered desirable properties of software design?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains both concepts
connects them to maintainability and testability
provides a reasonable module-level example

concepts: transactions preserve defined correctness guarantees across operations
questionPatterns: What does ACID stand for?
Why would transferring money between two accounts require a transaction?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains all four properties
connects atomicity to all-or-nothing execution
uses a practical multi-step operation example

questionPatterns: What problems do database isolation levels try to solve?
What's the tradeoff between stronger isolation and concurrency?
evaluationCriteria: understands isolation controls interactions between concurrent transactions
can explain common anomalies
recognizes stronger isolation can reduce concurrency or increase coordination cost

concepts: denormalization as a deliberate performance tradeoff
questionPatterns: Why do we normalize relational databases?
When might you intentionally denormalize data?
evaluationCriteria: connects normalization to reducing redundancy and anomalies
understands normalization is not simply splitting tables endlessly
recognizes denormalization can improve specific read patterns at the cost of duplication

concepts: additional data structure used to accelerate lookups
B-tree style indexes support ordered searches
indexes improve reads but add storage and write overhead
not every column should automatically be indexed
questionPatterns: How does a database index improve query performance?
Why shouldn't you create indexes on every column?
evaluationCriteria: understands indexes reduce the amount of data that may need to be scanned
recognizes write and storage tradeoffs
understands index usefulness depends on actual query patterns

concepts: combining related rows using join conditions
questionPatterns: What's the difference between an INNER JOIN and a LEFT JOIN?
Which join would you use if you wanted every user even when they had no orders?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains matching versus preserved rows
selects LEFT JOIN for preserving all rows from the left relation
can reason about missing matches

concepts: primary key uniquely identifies a row
foreign key references a key in another relation
questionPatterns: What's the difference between a primary key and a foreign key?
How do foreign keys help maintain data integrity?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains identification versus reference
connects foreign keys to referential integrity
can provide a simple relational example

topic: Big O Time and Space Complexity
concepts: worst-case analysis commonly expressed with Big O
constants and lower-order terms ignored asymptotically
questionPatterns: What does O(n log n) actually mean?
What's the time and space complexity of this algorithm?
evaluationCriteria: explains complexity as growth rather than exact runtime
distinguishes time from space complexity
can analyze simple loops and nested operations

concepts: average cost over a sequence of operations
individual operations may occasionally be expensive
dynamic array resizing as a classic example
questionPatterns: Why is appending to a dynamic array considered O(1) amortized if resizing can take O(n)?
What's the difference between amortized complexity and average-case complexity?
evaluationCriteria: explains spreading occasional expensive operations across many cheap ones
uses dynamic array growth correctly
does not confuse amortized analysis with probabilistic average-case analysis
followUpConcepts: Big O Time and Space Complexity

concepts: hash function maps keys to bucket locations
collisions are unavoidable in finite tables
collision handling through chaining or open addressing
average constant-time lookup under suitable assumptions
questionPatterns: How does a hash table achieve average O(1) lookup?
What happens when two keys produce the same hash?
evaluationCriteria: understands hashing does not eliminate collisions
explains at least one collision-resolution strategy
connects high load factor to degraded performance
followUpConcepts: Big O Time and Space Complexity

concepts: recursive function calls itself on smaller subproblems
each call creates a call frame
deep recursion can cause stack overflow
questionPatterns: What happens internally when a recursive function is called?
Why is a base case necessary?
evaluationCriteria: explains the role of the base case
connects recursive calls to call-stack growth
understands excessive recursion depth can exhaust stack space

concepts: divide problem into smaller independent subproblems
merge sort and binary search as examples
questionPatterns: What is the divide-and-conquer paradigm?
Why is merge sort considered divide and conquer?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains divide, solve and combine stages
provides a valid algorithm example
distinguishes independent subproblems from overlapping-subproblem dynamic programming

questionPatterns: When should you consider using dynamic programming?
What's the difference between memoization and tabulation?
evaluationCriteria: identifies overlapping subproblems and reusable results
correctly distinguishes top-down from bottom-up approaches
understands dynamic programming trades additional memory for avoiding repeated work

concepts: requires a correctness property specific to the problem
local optimum does not always produce global optimum
questionPatterns: What makes an algorithm greedy?
Why can't every optimization problem be solved using a greedy approach?
evaluationCriteria: explains local decision making
understands greedy correctness must be justified
can provide an example where greedy works or fails

concepts: BFS explores level by level using a queue
DFS explores deeply using recursion or a stack
BFS finds shortest path in an unweighted graph
space usage depends on graph shape
questionPatterns: What's the difference between BFS and DFS?
Which would you use to find the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
evaluationCriteria: correctly identifies queue versus stack behavior
selects BFS for unweighted shortest paths
understands traversal choice depends on problem requirements

concepts: space complexity depends on vertices and edges
adjacency lists are typically efficient for sparse graphs
matrices provide constant-time edge existence checks
questionPatterns: What's the difference between an adjacency list and adjacency matrix?
Which representation would you choose for a sparse graph?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains both representations
connects sparse graphs to adjacency-list space efficiency
recognizes adjacency matrices allow direct edge lookup

concepts: different traversal orders support different use cases
questionPatterns: What's the difference between preorder, inorder and postorder traversal?
Which traversal of a binary search tree produces sorted values?
evaluationCriteria: correctly states traversal orders
identifies inorder traversal for sorted BST output
understands level-order traversal uses BFS

concepts: left subtree contains smaller keys and right subtree larger keys under a chosen ordering rule
average search can be logarithmic when reasonably balanced
worst-case unbalanced tree can degrade to linear height
questionPatterns: What's the search complexity of a binary search tree?
Why can a BST degrade to O(n) lookup?
evaluationCriteria: distinguishes balanced and skewed cases
connects operation complexity to tree height
understands ordering enables directed search

concepts: heap maintains a partial ordering property
min heap exposes the smallest element
max heap exposes the largest element
priority queues are commonly implemented using heaps
insertion and removal of the top element are logarithmic
questionPatterns: What's the difference between a heap and a sorted array?
When would you use a priority queue?
evaluationCriteria: understands a heap is not globally sorted
correctly explains efficient access to the highest-priority element
provides a reasonable use case such as scheduling or top-k problems
followUpConcepts: Big O Time and Space Complexity

concepts: stack follows last in first out
queue follows first in first out
stacks support recursion and expression processing
queues support scheduling and breadth-first traversal
questionPatterns: What's the difference between a stack and a queue?
Why does BFS use a queue while DFS can use a stack?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains LIFO and FIFO behavior
connects data structure behavior to traversal order
provides practical use cases

concepts: linked lists use nodes connected by references
arrays typically have better cache locality
linked-list insertion can be constant time when the target node position is already known
questionPatterns: What's the difference between an array and a linked list?
Why are arrays often faster in practice despite some linked-list operations having better theoretical complexity?
evaluationCriteria: correctly compares access and insertion characteristics
mentions memory locality or pointer overhead
understands linked-list insertion complexity assumes the insertion location is already available
followUpConcepts: Big O Time and Space Complexity

concepts: compiler translates source into another executable or intermediate representation before or during execution pipeline
interpreter executes program instructions through a runtime
modern languages often combine compilation and interpretation techniques
questionPatterns: What's the difference between a compiler and an interpreter?
Is JavaScript purely interpreted?
evaluationCriteria: explains the conceptual distinction without oversimplifying modern runtimes
recognizes JIT compilation
understands many modern execution systems use hybrid approaches
